# 1) Here's one way to obtain the input data table

# input vectors
days = c("Sunday", "Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday")
social = c(107, 100, 46, 88, 117, 69, 98)
entertainment = c(29, 22, 32, 14, 0, 1, 0)
creativity = c(1, 3, 8, 1, 1, 1, 9)
other = c(101, 22, 69, 44, 72, 39, 46)

# assemble data frame
tbl <- data.frame(
  'Day' = days,
  'Social' = social,
  'Entertainment' = entertainment,
  'Creativity' = creativity,
  'Other' = other
)

# reshape into long (tall) format for ggplot convenience
tbl <- pivot_longer(
  data = tbl,            # table to be reshaped
  cols = Social:Other,   # variables to be "stacked" together
  names_to = "Category", # column-name of stacked variables
  values_to = "Time")    # column-name of stacked values

# convert time from minutes to hours
tbl <- mutate(tbl, Time = Time / 60)

head(tbl)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Day    Category        Time
  <chr>  <chr>          <dbl>
1 Sunday Social        1.78  
2 Sunday Entertainment 0.483 
3 Sunday Creativity    0.0167
4 Sunday Other         1.68  
5 Monday Social        1.67  
6 Monday Entertainment 0.367 




# another way to create a data tabe in "long" (tall) format
# for ggplot() convenience

Day <- rep(x = c("Sunday", 
                 "Monday", 
                 "Tuesday", 
                 "Wednesday", 
                 "Thursday",
                 "Friday", 
                 "Saturday"), 
           each = 4)

Category <- rep(x = c("Social", "Entertainment", "Creativity", "Other"), times = 7)

Time <- c(107, 29, 1, 101,
          100, 22, 3, 22,
          46, 32, 8, 69,
          88, 14, 1, 44,
          117, 0, 1, 72,
          69, 1, 1, 39,
          98, 0, 9, 46) / 60

tbl <- data.frame(Day, Category, Time)



# 2) Total time per day
# (to be used later to get average time)
dat_total_time = tbl %>% 
  group_by(Day) %>%
  summarise(total_time = sum(Time)) %>%
  ungroup()

dat_total_time

# A tibble: 7 × 2
  Day       total_time
  <chr>          <dbl>
1 Friday          1.83
2 Monday          2.45
3 Saturday        2.55
4 Sunday          3.97
5 Thursday        3.17
6 Tuesday         2.58
7 Wednesday       2.45




# 3) Barchart
# (code based on Angel Alcala's submission)

# color palette
orange1 = "#E59400"
light_gray1 = "#939393"
gray1 = "#4C4C4C"
blue1 = "#2D89E5"
light_blue1 = "#96C4F2"
green1 = "#00B300"


# barchart
ggplot(
  data = tbl, 
  aes(x = Day, 
      y = Time, 
      fill = factor(Category, levels = c("Other", "Creativity", "Entertainment","Social")))) +
  geom_bar(width = 0.6, position = "stack", stat = "identity") +
  # customizing scale elements
  scale_x_discrete(limits = days, labels = c("S", "M", "T", "W", "T", "F", "S")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = c("0", " ", " ", " ", "4h"), position = "right") + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(dat_total_time$total_time),
             color = green1,
             linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(gray1, 
                               orange1,
                               light_blue1,
                               blue1)) +
  # theme elements
  theme(panel.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = gray1),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_line(linetype = "dashed"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 15),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),
        legend.position = "none",
        plot.margin = unit(c(2.4,.7,2.4,.4), "cm"),
        axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        axis.title.y = element_blank()) +
  # annotations
  annotate("text", x = 1.3, y = 5.25, label = "Daily Average", size = 5.5, color = light_gray1) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.3, y = 4.6, label = "2h 38m", size = 10.5, color = "white") +
  annotate("text", x = 6.7, y = 4.5, label = "28% from last week", size = 5.5, color = light_gray1) + 
  annotate("text", x = 7.85, y = 2.75, label = "avg", size = 5, color = green1) +
  annotate("text", x = .96, y = -1.2, label = "Social", size = 6, color = blue1) +
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -1.2, label = "Entertainment", size = 6, color = light_blue1) + 
  annotate("text", x = 7.25, y = -1.2, label = "Creativity", size = 6, color = orange1) + 
  annotate("text", x = .96, y = -1.7, label = "10h 21m", size = 6, color = "white") +
  annotate("text", x = 4, y = -1.7, label = "1h 10m", size = 6, color = "white") + 
  annotate("text", x = 7.25, y = -1.7, label = "24m", size = 6, color = "white") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1,7), ylim = c(0,4), clip = "off")

title: 6) Color 2 - Use of Color
text:
In this week, we’ll continue talking about color, specifically about the use of color in data viz, and you will turn in HW4 (Friday 11:59pm).
In addition to color models (and their spaces), another important aspect of color has to do with its actual use when making graphics.
Recall that making graphics, conceptually speaking, is basically mapping data to geometric objects and their visual properties. One important property is color. Consequently we need to spend time talking about encoding data with color. Depending on the type of variables we are wroking with, and their associated measurement scales, colors can be used to encode such values. Therefore, we need to discuss which kinds of colors are more appropriate for encoding which types of variables.



At the end of this unit you will be able to:

Understand the default behavior of ggplot when a variable is mapped to a color attribute.
Indicate what color scale ggplot uses when you map a logical variable.
Indicate what color scale ggplot uses when you map an integer variable.
Indicate what color scale ggplot uses when you map a factor variable.
Indicate what color scale ggplot uses when you map a double variable.





Find today’s slides here.

title: Week 6 Homework
text:
In this assignment, you will recreate your first New York Times Data Visualization independently. The goal is not just to copy a graph, but to practice how we build visualizations step by step using the concepts we’ve learned in class, especially color mapping, scales, and core ggplot2 functions.  
 
You will take a real-world dataset and transform it into a meaningful visualization by thinking carefully about:

how variables are mapped to visual elements (like position, color, and size)
how color is used to represent different types of data and
how small design choices impact interpretation.

By the end of this assignment, you should feel more comfortable reading, modifying, and creating layered visualizations in R.

title: Week 6 Homework
text:
The Article
This assignment is inspired by a data visualization from The New York Times that explores global inequalities in COVID-19 vaccine distribution. The graphic compares countries based on wealth (GDP per capita) and vaccination rates, while also incorporating additional context like income groups and population.
 
Your task is to recreate a version of this visualization using real data.

title: Week 6 Homework
text:

title: Week 6 Homework
text:
The Data:
You will work with multiple real-world datasets from sources such as the World Bank and Our World in Data. These datasets include information on:

GDP per capita
COVID-19 vaccination rates
country income groups
and population

There are 4 main sources of data:

GDP per capita from the World Bank  https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.PCAP.CD
Vaccination data from Our World in Data  https://ourworldindata.org/search?q=vaccine%20doses
World Bank income groups, 1987 to 2022 from Our World in Data  https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/world-bank-income-groups?tab=table
Population by Country from the World Bank  https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.POP.TOTL

section: Part 4 - Merge GDP and Vaccination datasets
text:
Part 4 - Merge GDP and Vaccination datasets
Once we have the GDP data and the vaccination data, the next step involves merging them with inner_join(). This step is given to you because we have not covered joins in this course — essentially it combines two datasets into one by matching on a shared column.
gdp_vac = inner_join(dat_gdp_2021, dat_vac_2021, by = c("Country Code" = "Code"))

gdp_vac

title: Week 6 Homework
section: Part 5 - Prepare and merge Income Group data
text:
Part 5 - Prepare and merge Income Group data
Now we need to add income group labels to our dataset. The code below filters the income data to 2021, cleans up the labels, and merges it in. This step is also given to you.
# filter to 2021
dat_inc_2021 = filter(dat_inc, Year == 2021)

# clean up income labels (remove "-income countries" suffix)
dat_inc_2021$Income = str_remove(dat_inc_2021$Income, "-income countries")

# make Income an ordered factor so it displays correctly in plots
dat_inc_2021$Income = factor(
  x = dat_inc_2021$Income,
  levels = c("Low", "Lower-middle", "Upper-middle", "High"),
  ordered = TRUE
)

# merge with gdp_vac
gdp_vac_inc = inner_join(gdp_vac, dat_inc_2021, by = c("Country Code" = "Code"))

gdp_vac_inc

title: Week 6 Homework
section: Part 6 - Prepare and merge Population data
text:
Part 6 - Prepare and merge Population data
Finally, we add population data so we can size each point by country population.
# select Country Name, Country Code, and the 2021 population column
dat_pop_2021 = select(dat_pop, c(1:2, 66))
colnames(dat_pop_2021) = c("Country Name", "Country Code", "Population")

# merge with gdp_vac_inc
gdp_vac_inc_pop = inner_join(gdp_vac_inc, dat_pop_2021, by = "Country Code")

gdp_vac_inc_pop

title: Week 6 Homework
text:
Part 7 - Graphing
We will go over a series of plotting rounds, starting with a default scatter plot, and then gradually adding more elements, and customizing its appearance to get as close as possible to our target visualization.  

Create your first scatterplot using GDP and vaccination data  

You are mapping:  

GDP (wealth of a country) → x-axis
Vaccinations → y-axis

plot_a <- gdp_vac |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = ________, y = ________)) +
  geom_point()

Improve the x-axis  

Make the plot easier to read by applying a log transformation to GDP, as GDP varies a lot across countries. Log scales help us visualize large differences more clearly.
plot_b <- gdp_vac |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = log(________, base = ________),
           y = Vaccinations)) +
  geom_point()

Add labels  

Make your plot understandable by labeling the axes.
plot_c <- plot_b + labs(
  x = "________",
  y = "________"
)

Add income groups (color!)  

Add a categorical variable using color to map Income groups. This variable includes categories like Low, High, etc.
plot_d <- gdp_vac_inc |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = log(`2021`, base = 100),
           y = Vaccinations,
           color = ________)) +
  geom_point()

Customize colors  

Apply a custom color palette to control how colors appear in the chart. The income_colors vector below uses colors inspired by the original NYT graphic — feel free to experiment with ColorBrewer palettes as well!
income_colors = c(
  rgb(242, 129, 36,  maxColorValue = 255),  # Low
  rgb(249, 199, 158, maxColorValue = 255),  # Lower-middle
  rgb(166, 197, 198, maxColorValue = 255),  # Upper-middle
  rgb(54,  123, 127, maxColorValue = 255)   # High
)

plot_e <- plot_d + scale_color_manual(values = income_colors)

Add population (size)  

Add another variable to the plot. You are mapping population to the size of each point.
plot_f <- gdp_vac_inc_pop |>
    ggplot(aes(x = log(`2021`, base = 100),
           y = Vaccinations,
           color = Income,
           size = ________)) +
  geom_point()
Hint: The size of each bubble should represent the population of the country.

Adjust point sizes  

Make sizes visually reasonable: scale population so the points don’t become too large.
plot_g <- ____
Hint: use geom_point(size = sqrt(Population / 10000000)) instead of the default geom_point()

Final touches  

Improve the layout by adjusting where the legend appears. Look at the original NYT graph to identify where the legend should appear.
plot_h <- _____
Hint: use + theme(legend.position = "________")

title: Week 5
text:
Common purposes for color usage


Data encoding:
Design aspects:


Data encoding: A main use of color (although not the only one) is to encode data. This encoding chiefly depends on the type of variables we are working with (e.g. qualitative or quantitative, discrete or continuous). Design aspects: Another important use of color has to do with design considerations such as psychology of color, decorative purposes, harmony, and things like that.

title: Week 5
section: How do we specify color in a computational sense?
text:
How do we specify color in a computational sense?

How do we tell a computer program what kind of color we want to use? The answer to this question is given by Color Models, and their associated color spaces (or geometric volumes).

Red, Green, and Blue light sources are combined to display colors on televisions and computer monitors.

text:
Part 2: Single Variable Distributions
6. Create a bar plot of species.


Use geom_bar().

Does this geom require you to count manually?



penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species)) +
  geom_bar()








Written answer here: no, this geom automatically counts the species up
7. Create a bar plot of island.


Which island appears most common?


penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar()








Written answer here: Biscoe appears most often.
8. Create a histogram of bill_length_mm.


Does the distribution look symmetric, skewed left, or skewed right?


penguins |>
    ggplot(aes(x= bill_length_mm)) +
    geom_histogram()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.


Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).









Written answer here: The distribution is mostly symmetric with an obvious dip in the middle range
9. Create a density plot of bill_length_mm.


Use geom_density().

How does it compare to the histogram?



penguins |>
    ggplot(aes(x= bill_length_mm)) +
    geom_density()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_density()`).









Written answer here: The density plot mostly maintains the same structure, but represents the density through a smooth line rather than through bars
10. Create a boxplot of body_mass_g.


Are there potential outliers?


penguins |>
    ggplot(aes(x= body_mass_g)) +
    geom_boxplot()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).









Written answer here: Most values seem centered around 3500 to 4800. There is a large range, so there are potentially a lot of outliers.

title: Week 4 Activity - Solutions
text:
Part 3: Grouped Summaries + geom_col()
11. Count the number of penguins in each species using count(). Store the result.

species_count <- penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarize(count = n())
# Adelie has most penguins

12. Use geom_col() to recreate the species counts.


Why must we use geom_col() instead of geom_bar() here?



penguins |>
    count(species) |>
    ggplot(aes(y = species, x = n)) +
    geom_col()








Written answer here: We must use geom col because it is able to draw up the lines to a pre-defined count, whereas using geom_bar is used for raw data. ggplot would try to count the rows of the summarized data, which would give each species a count of 1 (because each appears once after count()).
13. Count the number of penguins by species and island.


Which combination is most common?



penguins |>
  count(species, island) |>
  arrange(desc(n))

# A tibble: 5 × 3
  species   island        n
  <fct>     <fct>     <int>
1 Gentoo    Biscoe      124
2 Chinstrap Dream        68
3 Adelie    Dream        56
4 Adelie    Torgersen    52
5 Adelie    Biscoe       44


Written answer here: The most common combination is Gentoo penguins on Biscoe Island

title: Week 4 Activity - Solutions
text:
Part 4: Two Variable Relationships
14. Create a scatterplot of:


bill_length_mm (x)
flipper_length_mm (y)
What kind of relationship do you see?


penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_point()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Written answer here: It looks like, as bill length increases, so does flipper length. There is a positive correlation between the two variables.
15. Modify the scatterplot:


Add color = species.

Does separating by species clarify the relationship?


penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, 
             y = flipper_length_mm, 
             color = species)) +
  geom_point()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Written answer here: Yes, it makes the relationship very clear now because it separates the clouds of points. It becomes very obvious that the species that the penguin is has some sort of impact or causal effect on the flipper and bill length.
16. Change the same scatterplot to: 

shape = species instead of color.

When might shape be preferable to color?


penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, 
             y = flipper_length_mm, 
             shape = species)) +
  geom_point()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Written answer here: Shape might be preferable to color in black-white printing, if someone is color blind, if you want to use color for another variable (like sex), etc
17. Create a boxplot of: body_mass_g by species. 

Which species tends to be heaviest?


penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_boxplot()

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).









Written answer here: Gentoo tends to be heaviest.

title: Week 4 Activity - Solutions
text:
Part 5: Reflection (Short Discussion)
18. What is the difference between geom_bar() and geom_col()?

Written answer here: Geom_bar is for when raw data needs summarizing because the default is count, whereas col is better for pre-summarized data.
19. What is the unit of observation in:


penguins?
mtcars?

Written answer here: In penguins, each row is a penguin on the island. In mtcars, each row is a car model from a specific brand.
20. Which plots felt more exploratory vs explanatory?
Written answer here: up to interpretation, but generally plots where we summarize or highlight a message are explanatory, while plots that let us probe and investigate patterns are exploratory. 15, 16, 17 are examples of explanatory charts, and 6-10 are examples of exploratory plots.
21. Next level
Let’s practice making some graphs with ggplot2!

Load the mpg dataset (which is inside tidyverse package).


mpg

   manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy fl    class

 2 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 p     comp…
 3 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 p     comp…
 4 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 p     comp…

 7 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 p     comp…

 9 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 p     comp…
10 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 p     comp…
# ℹ 224 more rows



Take a look at the data: what is easy column representing? What does each row represent? (Hint: if you are not sure, type ?mpg into your console to launch the help panel)


?mpg

starting httpd help server ... done

# Each row is a car model
# each column is a parameter about that car
# manufacturer = manufacturer name
# model = model name
# displ = engine displacement, in litres
# year = year of manufacture
# cyl = number of cylinders
# trans = type of transmission
# drv = the type of drive train, where f = front-wheel drive, r = rear wheel drive, 4 = 4wd
# cty = city miles per gallon
# hwy = highway miles per gallon
# fl = fuel type
# class = "type" of car


Create a scatter plot of model vs cty, colored by year.


mpg |>
    ggplot(aes(x = model, y = cty, color = year)) +
    geom_point()

title: Week 3
text:
Data types in R
Data Types: A data type tells you what kind of data something is, like a number, a word (string), or a TRUE/FALSE value (logical).


# Numeric (numbers)
num <- 42

# Character (text)
text <- "Hello, R!"

# Logical (TRUE/FALSE)
logic <- TRUE

# Print the values and their types
print(num)      # 42


[1] 42

print(text)     # "Hello, R!"

[1] "Hello, R!"

print(logic)    # TRUE

[1] TRUE

# Check the data type using class()
class(num)   # "numeric"

[1] "numeric"

class(text)  # "character"

[1] "character"

class(logic) # "logical"

[1] "logical"

title: Week 3
text:
Data Pipelines in R



2016: magrittr introduces %>%

|>
2021:

R now provides a simple native forward pipe syntax |>. The simple form of the forward pipe inserts the left-hand side as the first argument in the right-hand side call.

title: Week 3
text:
Using pipeline with our data
Without the pipeline, we would have to apply each function one at a time:

summarize(
  filter(data, major == "statistics"),
  mean(sleep_hours, na.rm = TRUE)
)
# results in nested function which can be very messy




Using the pipeline, this becomes very clean:

data |>
  filter(major == "statistics") |>
  summarise(mean(sleep_hours, na.rm = TRUE))

  seasonal_worker = c("yes", "TRUE", "no", "Yes", "false", "TRUE"),
  supervisor = c("no", "no", "TRUE", "no", "TRUE", "no"),
  commute_min = c("15 minutes", "35 mins", "20min", "50 minute", "10minutes", "40mins"),
  primary_crop = c(" Corn", "wheat ", "CORN", "Soy", " soy ", "WHEAT"),
  years_experience = c("2 years", "5rs", "10 yrs", "3 yr", "12 years", "4yr"),
  hourly_wage = c("18 dollars", "20/hr", "25 per hour", "19 dollars", "28 usd", "21 /day")
)

title: Week 3
text:
Data Cleaning Example:

library(tidyverse)

farm_clean <- farm_raw |>
  mutate(
    # numeric columns
    hours_worked = parse_number(hours_worked),
    commute_min = parse_number(commute_min),
    years_experience = parse_number(years_experience),
    hourly_wage = parse_number(hourly_wage),

    # yes/no variables
    seasonal_worker = case_when(
      str_to_lower(str_trim(seasonal_worker)) %in% c("yes", "true") ~ TRUE,
      str_to_lower(str_trim(seasonal_worker)) %in% c("no", "false") ~ FALSE,
      TRUE ~ NA
    ),

    supervisor = case_when(
      str_to_lower(str_trim(supervisor)) %in% c("yes", "true") ~ TRUE,
      str_to_lower(str_trim(supervisor)) %in% c("no", "false") ~ FALSE,
      TRUE ~ NA
    ),

    # categorical cleanup
    primary_crop =
      primary_crop |>
      str_trim() |>
      str_to_lower()
  )

farm_clean

title: Week 2
text:
Categorical
Type of variable that represents categories or groups. It takes one of a limited, usually fixed, number of possible values, assigning each unit of observation to a particular group or nominal category on the basis of some qualitative property.
Examples:

Binary: Only takes two possible values, and are typically represented as 1 or 0.
Nominal: Categories have no clear order, and are mutually exclusive
Ordinal: Have a clear ordering of the categories

title: Week 2
text:
Quantitative
Numerical data point (AKA numerical variable) that can be measured or counted, allowing for mathematical operations to be applied.
Examples:

Discrete: Can take any countable value represented as an integer (whole number),
Continuous: Can take any measurable value within a given range, including fractions and decimals.

title: Week 2
text:
“The Grammar of Graphics”
From a conceptual perspective, making graphics involves mapping data to geometric objects and their visual properties.

title: Week 2
section: Why do we need Grammar of Graphics (GG)?
text:
Why do we need Grammar of Graphics (GG)?

We believe that the GG is a good starting point that gives us a framework (mental map) and a vocabulary to create graphics.
This grammar allows creation of graphics to be:

consistent
reusable
modular

title: Week 2
text:
Formalized by Leland Wilkinson (1999)



A formal set of rules to create any type of graphics
Underlies most modern data visualization software.
Implemented as an R package (‘ggplot2’)

title: Week 2
text:
Key Concepts



Data: the data set being visualized (next week!)
Aesthetics (aes): how variables map to visual properties (x, y, color, size, etc)
Geometric objects (geoms): what kind of marks to draw (points, bars, lines)
Facets: creating small multiples by splitting data into panels
Stats: statistical transformations (e.g. binning for histograms, smoothing lines)
Coordinates: how data values are translated into visual values (e.g. color gradients)
Themes: controlling non-data elements (fonts, backgrounds, gridlines)

title: Week 2
text:
Aesthetic Mapping

An aesthetic mapping links a variable in the data to a visual channel that can encode its variation.


Channels for ordered variables




From “Data Visualization” by Kieran Healy.

title: Week 2
text:
Channels for unordered variables








From “Data Visualization” by Kieran Healy.

title: Week 2
text:
Geometry
The geometry describes how to translate the observations into marks on the page.

Examples

Point
Line
Bar

title: Week 2
text:
ggplot2()

A plot can be decomposed into three primary elements:

the data,
the aesthetic mapping of the variables in the data to visual channels, and
the geometry used to translate the observations into marks on the plot.

title: Week 1
text:
Some example graphs

# graph 1 - sleep vs commute time (scatter plot with regression line)
graph1 <- ggplot(sleep_data, aes(x = commute_min, y = sleep_hours)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep vs Commute Time",
    x = "Commute Time (minutes)",
    y = "Average Weekday Sleep (hours)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# graph 2 - sleep vs favorite drink (bar chart)
graph2 <- sleep_data %>%
  group_by(favorite_drink) %>%
  summarise(avg_sleep = mean(sleep_hours)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = favorite_drink, y = avg_sleep)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Sleep by Favorite Drink",
    x = "Favorite Drink",
    y = "Average Sleep (hours)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# graph 3 - sleep vs units (scatter plot colored by transfer status)
graph3 <- ggplot(sleep_data, aes(x = units, y = sleep_hours, color = transfer)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep vs Units by Transfer Status",
    x = "Units Enrolled",
    y = "Average Weekday Sleep (hours)",
    color = "Transfer Student"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# graph 4 - sleep vs major (boxplot colored by grad student status)
graph4 <- ggplot(sleep_data, aes(x = major, y = sleep_hours, fill = grad_student)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Sleep Hours by Major",
    x = "Major",
    y = "Average Weekday Sleep (hours)",
    fill = "Grad Student"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: About
text: This is the website for the Spring-2026 DeCal course Concepts of Data Visualization in R.

title: About
text:
Motivation
Data visualization is an ever-present element for anyone who works with data, whether in business, science, education, politics, or any other field.
As data visualization expert Donna Wong says:

“The ability to create effective charts and graphs has become almost as indispensable as good writing.”

The good news is that, nowadays, making graphics, plots, diagrams, charts, etc has become incredibly easy. However, data visualization is not just about knowing how to use tools or software to produce a graphic. Quoting Donna Wong again:

“With computer technology, anyone can create graphics, but few of us know how to do it well.”

Data visualization is also about having a critical mindset, and acquiring the training not just to produce effective graphics, but also to learn how to assess a visual display of data.
To us, data visualization entails both the production as well as the consumption of graphics. Those of us who deal with data, sooner or later have to produce charts and graphs, and we have to be prepared to do this task effectively. Likewise, some of us will have to create graphics on a constant basis, some periodically, and some very rarely. However, all of us will be consuming visual displays of data. Therefore, we also need to be able to critically assess a graphic, and also know how to do it well.
In summary, our aim is to provide students the fundamental principles to:

Produce effective graphics, and
Assess the effectiveness of a visual display of data.

title: Syllabus
Syllabus
Syllabus
text:
§ Learning Outcomes
At the end of the course students will be able to:

Explain the importance/need of visualizing data.
Describe the framework of the grammar of graphics for creating plots.
Use functions from ggplot2 and other packages for making graphics in R.
Specify colors through the use of a color model, and its hexadecimal syntax.
Demonstrate an adequate encoding of data with color.
Recognize color schemes when inspecting a graphic.
List five or more of the analytical patterns/tasks (proposed by Stephen Few) for choosing the right graphic.
Apply and/or suggest changes that increase the data-to-ink ratio of a graphic.
Assess the effectiveness of a graphic intended for reporting/communicating purposes.
Produce animated charts as well as graphics with interactive elements.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Prerequisites
Because our main computational tool is R (and RStudio), it is desirable to have taken a course in which you got an introduction to R (e.g. STAT 20, 33A, 33B, 133; PB HLTH 142). It is also nice to have some previous experience analyzing data. Being concurrently enrolled in these classes is also acceptable.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Methods of Instruction
We will be using a combination of materials such as slides, tutorials, sample scripts, reading assignments, live demos, and chalk-and-talk. In-class attendance is mandatory. The main computational tool will be the computing and programming environment R. The main workbench will be the IDE RStudio.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Textbooks
The following books are not required, but will be referenced throughout the course for slides, assignments, and more. They can all be found online:

Data Visualization by K. Healy
Geocomputation with R by Lovelace, J. Nowosad, and J. Muenchow.
Now You See It: Simple Visualization Techniques for Quantitative Analysis by S. Few

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Grading

In-Class Worksheets (40%) (1 drop)
Quizzes (20%) (1 drop)
Homeworks (20%)
Final Project (20%)

Quizzes and the Final Project will be graded on accuracy. Homeworks and Worksheets are graded credit/no credit, where credit is given for a genuine attempt at the problems.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Assignments - Homeworks
Homework assignments will give the students a platform to demonstrate and reinforce concepts covered during lectures.
HWs will generally be assigned every week (except quiz weeks).
Refer to homework overviews for instructions on submitting.
You must write your own answers (using your own words, and your coding style—see Academic Honesty policy below).

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Assignments - Readings
There will also be weekly readings, mostly consisting of articles from The New York Times to analyze them for data visualization techniques. These will be discussed at the beginning of class and worksheets will contain reading-related questions.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Attendance
Attendance will be taken each lecture, with one unexcused absence allowed.
In case of emergency, contact instructors for excused absences. You will still be expected to complete the class work from that date and email the instructors your work.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Learning Cooperatively
With the obvious exception of quizzes, we encourage you to discuss all of the course activities with your friends and classmates as you are working on them. You will definitely learn more in this class if you work with others than if you do not. Ask questions, answer questions, and share ideas liberally.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Academic Honesty
Cooperation has a limit, however. You should not share your code or answers directly with other students. Doing so doesn’t help them; it just sets them up for trouble. Feel free to discuss approaches to the problems with others, but you must write your own solutions. Please complete your own work and keep it to yourself. If you suspect other people may be plagiarizing you, let us know ASAP. For more information please read the Honor Code Guide for Syllabi.
We expect you to do your own work and to uphold the standards of intellectual integrity. Collaborating on homework is fine and I encourage you to work together—but copying is not, nor is having somebody else submit assignments for you. Cheating will not be tolerated. Anyone found cheating will receive an NP grade and will be reported to the Center for the Student Conduct).
As for AI, our policy is that it is acceptable to use as a tool and even encouraged occasionally, as long as it does not interfere with your learning of the materials. We encourage its use as a tutor or for small questions, but not for writing entire pieces of code. You will be quizzed in class and those quizzes will be a large portion of your grade, so understanding the material and being able to synthesize code on your own is essential.
If you are having trouble with an assignment or studying for an exam, or if you are uncertain about permissible and impermissible conduct or collaboration, please come see me with your questions. Rather than copying someone else’s work, ask for help. You are not alone in this course! If you invest the time to learn the material and complete the projects, you won’t need to copy any answers.

title: Syllabus
text:
§ Special Accommodations
Students needing accommodations for any physical, psychological, or learning disability, should speak with me during the first two weeks of the semester, either after or during class and see http://dsp.berkeley.edu to learn about Berkeley’s policy.
If you are a DSP student, please contact me as soon as possible so that we can work out acceptable accommodations.
For relevant DSP accommodations that provide occasional extensions on assignments, we may provide a two-day extension as long as you contact us before the assignment is due. More details about these considerations may be discussed with the DSP staff.
If you are an athlete or Cal band member, please check your calendar. Do not take the class if you are not available to attend 90% of classes.

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
Agenda
Two big ideas today:

Data Pipelines
Grouped Operations


Before class, sketch out the effect that the different dplyr verbs from last class have on the shape of a data frame.

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
library(dplyr)
star_wars <- data.frame(
    name = c("Anakin", "Padme", "Luke", "JarJar"),
    homeworld = c("Tatooine", "Naboo", "Tatooine", "Naboo"),
    height = c(1.8, 1.6, 1.7, 1.9),
    weight = c(84, 45, 77, 90)
)

Using base R data frame subsetting, create from star_wars…

The average height of characters from Tatooine



Let’s look at three ways to solve this.

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
Nesting

summarize(filter(star_wars, homeworld == "Tatooine"), mean(height))



  mean(height)
1         1.75






Must be read from the inside out 👎
Hard to keep track of arguments 👎



All in one line of code 👍
Only refer to one data frame 👍

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
Step-by-step

star_wars2 <- filter(star_wars, homeworld == "Tatooine")
summarize(star_wars2, mean(height))



  mean(height)
1         1.75






Have to repeaet data frame names 👎
Creates unnecessary objects 👎



Stores intermediate objects 👍
Can be read from top to bottom 👍

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
Data Pipelines in R



2016: magrittr introduces %>%

|>
2021:

R now provides a simple native forward pipe syntax |>. The simple form of the forward pipe inserts the left-hand side as the first argument in the right-hand side call.

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
Using the Pipe Operator

star_wars |>

title: Data Wrangling II
Data Wrangling II
text:
Using the Pipe Operator

star_wars |>
    filter(homeworld == "Tatooine") |>
    summarize(mean(height))



  mean(height)
1         1.75






🤷‍♂️



Can be read like an english paragraph 👍
Only type the data once 👍
No leftover objects 👍

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
Understanding your pipeline
It’s good practice to understand the output of each line by breaking the pipe.




star_wars |>
    select(homeworld) |>
    filter(mean(height))



Error in `filter()`:
ℹ In argument: `mean(height)`.
Caused by error:
! object 'height' not found




star_wars |>
    select(homeworld)



  homeworld
1  Tatooine
2     Naboo
3  Tatooine
4     Naboo

title: Data Wrangling II
Data Wrangling II
text:
|> works everywhere

R now provides a simple native forward pipe syntax |>. The simple form of the forward pipe inserts the left-hand side as the first argument in the right-hand side call.



factor(c("cat", "cat", "dog")) |> summary()



cat dog 
  2   1

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
library(dplyr)
star_wars <- data.frame(
    name = c("Anakin", "Padme", "Luke", "JarJar"),
    homeworld = c("Tatooine", "Naboo", "Tatooine", "Naboo"),
    height = c(1.8, 1.6, 1.7, 1.9),
    weight = c(84, 45, 77, 90)
)

Using base R data frame subsetting, create from star_wars…

The average height of characters from Tatooine across each planet



You could set up two pipelines with different filters. But there’s a better way.

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by()
Flag the rows of a dataframe as belong to a group defined by a factor. For use in downstream operations.


star_wars |>
    group_by(homeworld)



# A tibble: 4 × 4
# Groups:   homeworld [2]
  name   homeworld height weight
  <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Anakin Tatooine     1.8     84
2 Padme  Naboo        1.6     45
3 Luke   Tatooine     1.7     77
4 JarJar Naboo        1.9     90

title: Data Wrangling II
Data Wrangling II
text: Draw diagram

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by() with summarize()


star_wars |>
    summarize(mean(height))



  mean(height)
1         1.75

title: Data Wrangling II
Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by() with filter()


star_wars |>
    filter(height == max(height))



    name homeworld height weight
1 JarJar     Naboo    1.9     90

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by() with filter()

star_wars |>
    group_by(homeworld) |>
    filter(height == max(height))



# A tibble: 2 × 4
# Groups:   homeworld [2]
  name   homeworld height weight
  <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Anakin Tatooine     1.8     84
2 JarJar Naboo        1.9     90




Changes the scope of functions inside filter() to operate within groups.


Can bring up drop to demonstrate how a condition like height > 1.7 will operate the same with or without the grouping.

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by() with arrange()


star_wars |>
    arrange(desc(height))



    name homeworld height weight
1 JarJar     Naboo    1.9     90
2 Anakin  Tatooine    1.8     84
3   Luke  Tatooine    1.7     77
4  Padme     Naboo    1.6     45

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by() with arrange()

star_wars |>
    group_by(homeworld) |>
    arrange(desc(height))



# A tibble: 4 × 4
# Groups:   homeworld [2]
  name   homeworld height weight
  <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>
1 JarJar Naboo        1.9     90
2 Anakin Tatooine     1.8     84
3 Luke   Tatooine     1.7     77
4 Padme  Naboo        1.6     45




Arrange ignores group_by() and is always global

Why???

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
star_wars |>
    arrange(homeworld, height)



    name homeworld height weight
1  Padme     Naboo    1.6     45
2 JarJar     Naboo    1.9     90
3   Luke  Tatooine    1.7     77
4 Anakin  Tatooine    1.8     84

title: Data Wrangling II
text:
group_by() with mutate()


star_wars |>
    mutate(height_z = (height - mean(height)) / sd(height))



    name homeworld height weight   height_z
1 Anakin  Tatooine    1.8     84  0.3872983
2  Padme     Naboo    1.6     45 -1.1618950
3   Luke  Tatooine    1.7     77 -0.3872983
4 JarJar     Naboo    1.9     90  1.1618950

text:

star_wars |>
    group_by(homeworld) |>
    mutate(height_z = (height - mean(height)) / sd(height))

# Groups:   homeworld [2]
  name   homeworld height weight height_z
  <chr>  <chr>      <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl>

Changes the scope of functions inside mutate() to operate within groups.

title: Data Wrangling II

Data Wrangling II
text:
Shortcut: .by
Most dplyr functions have a .by argument to apply the function to the groups of another variable.

Not stateful
Good for single line operations



star_wars |>
    summarize(mean(height), .by = homeworld)



  homeworld mean(height)
1  Tatooine         1.75
2     Naboo         1.75

Explanatory: - consumer: stakeholder - goal: tell a specific story - customized - polished aesthetic

title: Week 4
text:
ggplot2()

A plot can be decomposed into three primary elements (according to grammar of graphics):

the data,
the aesthetic mapping of the variables in the data to visual channels, and
the geometry used to translate the observations into marks on the plot.

title: Week 4
text:
ggplot2() syntax

ggplot2() builds a plot layer by layer, each one added on top of one another with + (not |>).



ggplot(df) creates canvas
aes() creates mappings, called inside ggplot() or a geom()
geom_() puts down marks using declared geometry

title: Week 4

Layer by layer

penguins |>
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm,
             y = flipper_length_mm,
             color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_gray(base_size = 18)

title: Lecture 6
section: Why Color Matters in Data Visualization
text:
Why Color Matters in Data Visualization
Color is one of the most powerful visual tools in a graphic.

It helps us distinguish groups, highlight patterns, and encode information.
But before deciding how to use color, we need to understand what type of data we are visualizing.
In visualization, data typically falls into two main categories:

title: Lecture 6
text:
Main categories

Categorical (Qualitative) – represents groups or labels



Binary (two categories)

Nominal (categories with no order)

Ordinal (categories with a meaningful order)



Quantitative (Numerical) – represents measurable values



Discrete (countable values)

Continuous (values on a continuous scale)

title: Lecture 6
section: Is A and B the same color?
text: Is A and B the same color?

title: Lecture 6
text:
Why Color Perception Matters
Demo
This famous illusion demonstrates that our perception of color is influenced by surrounding context. Even when two squares are the exact same color, our brain interprets them differently because of lighting and shadow.

If color is not chosen carefully, viewers may misinterpret differences in the data.

title: Lecture 6
text:
Why Color Choice Matters
Human color perception is relative and context-dependent.
The Adelson Checker Shadow Illusion shows that the same color can appear different depending on its surroundings.
In visualizations, non-uniform color scales (like rainbow) can distort patterns:


Some transitions appear more dramatic than others

Middle values may seem more important due to bright colors


Choosing the right color scale is critical for accurate data interpretation.

title: Lecture 6
text:
Let’s Look at ColorBrewer
We will use ColorBrewer as a tool to explore different color palettes designed specifically for visualizing different types of data.
ColorBrewer Website ColorBrewer organizes color palettes into three main types:


Qualitative: categorical variables (unordered)
Sequential: ordered categorical, numeric variables (single direction)
Diverging: numeric variables (numeric variables with meaningful midpoint)

title: Lecture 6
section: Some R Packages for Color Palettes
text:
Some R Packages for Color Palettes
Let’s look at the datanovia website!

Viridis color scales [viridis package]
Colorbrewer palettes [RColorBrewer package]
Grey color palettes [ggplot2 package]
Scientific journal color palettes [ggsci package]
Wes Anderson color palettes [wesanderson package]
R base color palettes: rainbow, heat.colors, cm.colors.

Warning: not all palettes are perceptually uniform!

title: Lecture 6
text:
Perceptually Uniform Color Scales
Perceptually uniform scales ensure that equal changes in data correspond to equal visual differences in color.
Benefits:
- Accurate data interpretation: patterns are represented faithfully
- Avoids visual misrepresentation: no unintended emphasis on certain ranges
- Inclusivity & accessibility: many scales are colorblind-friendly, greyscale-printable, and intuitive

If a color scale isn’t uniform, it might make some values look more important than they actually are, or hide patterns that are there. So you end up telling a misleading story with your graph—even if the data is correct.

title: Lecture 6
section: In ggplot2, color palettes are applied using scale_color_*() functions.
text:
In ggplot2, color palettes are applied using scale_color_*() functions.
These functions control how values in a variable are mapped to colors in a plot.

Important points: - scale_color_*() is where palettes are applied
- Different palettes come from different R packages
- Changing the palette does not change the data or the plot structure
- It only changes the visual style of the graphic

title: Lecture 6
text:
Example using packages

#Simple example, don't forget to install packages :)
ggplot(data, aes(x, y, color = group)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_viridis_d()

title: Lecture 6
text:
How ggplot assignes color
When you map a variable to the color aesthetic, ggplot automatically chooses colors depending on the data type of that variable:



Data Type
Default Color Behavior




Logical (TRUE/FALSE)
Two colors (discrete)


Integer / Double
Gradient (continuous)


Factor
Discrete palette


Character
Discrete palette

title: Lecture 6
text:
Demo Dataset
Let’s create a small data frame with several variable types.


library(ggplot2)
set.seed(123)

demo_df <- data.frame(
  x = rnorm(20),
  y = rnorm(20),
  logical_var = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), 20, replace = TRUE),
  integer_var = sample(1:5, 20, replace = TRUE),
  double_var = runif(20, 0, 10),
  factor_var = factor(sample(letters[1:3], 20, replace = TRUE)),
  char_var = sample(c("cat", "dog", "bird"), 20, replace = TRUE)
)

text:

title: Lecture 6
text:

title: Lecture 6
text:
Activity: Customize the Color Scales
Recreate the plots, but use a color scale function to control the palette.
Experiment with both discrete and continuous color scales.

# Discrete manual colors for factor or character variables
ggplot(demo_df, aes(x, y, color = char_var)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("cat" = "orange", "dog" = "steelblue", "bird" = "forestgreen")) +
  labs(title = "Custom Discrete Colors")









# Continuous gradient for numeric variable
ggplot(demo_df, aes(x, y, color = double_var)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  scale_color_continuous(low = "lightblue", high = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Custom Continuous Scale")







# Using scale_fill_discrete() with filled geoms
ggplot(demo_df, aes(x = factor_var, fill = char_var)) +
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_discrete() +
  labs(title = "Bar Plot with Discrete Fill")

title: Lecture 7
text:
What Just Happened?

Change blindness: our brains don’t store a perfect snapshot of the world
We only process what we’re focused on or what jumps out
Small changes in unattended regions are completely invisible
This demonstrates the power and limits of human visual perception







Tip


Takeaway for dataviz: If something is important, you can’t rely on your audience to find it. You must direct their attention to it.

title: Lecture 7
text:
How the Visual System Works




Visual processing has two components:
Eyes — image receptors

Capture raw pixel information
No interpretation yet

Brain — image processor + interpreter

Imposes structure and meaning
Fills in gaps, groups objects, detects patterns
Decides what to pay attention to

title: Lecture 7
text:
Visual Memory: Three Stages







Memory Type
Analogy
Duration




Iconic memory
Buffer / temp storage
< 0.5 sec


Short-term (working)
RAM
~20 sec


Long-term
Hard disk
Permanent



Good visualizations work with these limits — not against them.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Iconic Memory = Preattentive Processing

Iconic memory is a waiting room where each snapshot waits to be passed to short-term memory.


Processing is rapid (under 250ms)
It is parallel so you see the whole image at once, not pixel by pixel
It is unconscious, happens before you “decide” to look
It relies on contrast between features

title: Lecture 7
text:
The Power of Preattentive Processing
Can you count the 6s?

title: Lecture 7
text:
The Power of Preattentive Processing
Now try:


What changed? A single visual attribute, color, made the target pop out without counting.
This is preattentive processing at work.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Two Types of Visual Search



Preattentive (fast)
Attentive (slow)




Parallel — whole image at once
Serial — scan one element at a time


< 250ms regardless of # items
Time increases with number of items


“Pop-out” effect
Effortful, exhausting

title: Lecture 7
text:
The Preattentive Attributes
These visual features are processed before conscious attention:


Form

Shape
Size
Orientation
Line length / width
Enclosure / grouping
Added marks (fill, texture)


Color

Hue (the “color” itself)
Intensity / saturation
Luminance / brightness

Spatial

2D position
Depth cues (stereoscopic)
Motion

title: Lecture 7
text:
Activity: Spot the Preattentive Attribute
Which attribute makes the target pop out instantly? Which requires search?
Try each example below and think about why it’s fast or slow.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Design Rule #1






Use preattentive attributes to direct attention


If something is the most important part of your chart, an outlier, a threshold, a key category, encode it with a preattentive attribute (usually color or size) so it pops out without effort.
Don’t make your audience search for the point.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Design Rule #2






Don’t overload the preattentive channels


Using too many colors, sizes, or shapes forces your viewer into attentive (slow) search mode, exactly what you were trying to avoid.
Rule of thumb: highlight one thing with a strong preattentive signal. Keep everything else neutral (gray is your friend).

title: Lecture 7
text:
Memory and Visualization
Understanding how our brains pay attention, and how our memory system handles incoming information, goes a long way toward making better visualizations.
Key question to ask yourself every time: “Am I making my audience work harder than necessary?”

title: Lecture 7
text:
What is Gestalt Theory?
Gestalt (German: “whole” or “form”)

Developed in Germany in the 1920s
A theory of form processing:how we organize visual input into meaningful wholes
The whole is different from the sum of its parts
We don’t see isolated pixels but rather objects, groups, and relationships

Good design works with these principles

title: Lecture 7
text:
The 8 Main Gestalt Principles



Principle
The Brain Assumes…




Proximity
Things close together belong together


Similarity
Things that look alike are related


Continuity
Lines and curves continue in the smoothest path


Closure
Incomplete shapes are seen as complete


Connectivity
Objects joined by lines are related


Enclosure
Objects sharing a boundary form a group


Symmetry
Symmetric arrangements feel stable and grouped


Figure & Ground
We separate foreground from background

title: Lecture 7
text:
Gestalt in practice: FiveThirtyEight
We’ll walk through several published charts and identify the Gestalt principles at work.

title: Lecture 7
section: Why Democrats Struggle to Mobilize a Religious Left
text: Why Democrats Struggle to Mobilize a Religious Left

title: Lecture 7
text:
Principle: Connectivity


In dataviz: Lines in a line chart connect points across time. The connection implies continuity and relationship even though the data points are discrete.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Principle: Proximity


In dataviz: Grouped bar charts use proximity to show that bars within a group belong together (same time period, same category). Spacing between groups signals separation.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Principle: Similarity


In dataviz: A consistent color for “Democrats” across all charts in a report uses similarity to signal the same category.

title: Lecture 7
section: Bryce Harper May Already Be Past His Prime
text:
Bryce Harper May Already Be Past His Prime




Principle: Continuity

The eye follows the smoothest path through a series of points so we perceive a trend, not individual values, and this is why line charts are so good at showing trajectories

title: Lecture 7
text: How Unpopular is Donald Trump?

title: Lecture 7
text:
Chunking

Chunking is the process of grouping individual pieces of information into meaningful wholes to improve retention.


Working memory can hold ~4 “chunks” at a time
Good charts chunk information visually (through spacing, color, enclosure, or labels)

title: Lecture 7
section: The 5 Corners of the 2020 Democratic Primary
text: The 5 Corners of the 2020 Democratic Primary

title: Lecture 7
text:
Principle: Enclosure
Objects sharing a common region are perceived as belonging together.


In dataviz: Shaded regions, background panels, and borders create groups without explicit labels. The boundary does the cognitive work for you.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Principle: Figure & Ground
We instinctively separate a “figure” (foreground object) from its “ground” (background).


In dataviz: A white chart background is the “ground.” Your data marks are the “figure.” Gridlines should recede into the background, not compete with your data.
High-contrast accent colors push elements into the foreground.

title: Lecture 7
text:
Putting It All Together






A checklist for every chart you make



Is the most important element highlighted with a preattentive attribute?
Is everything else visually muted (gray, thin, light)?
Are related elements close together (proximity)?
Are same-category elements visually consistent (similarity)?
Are groups enclosed or separated with whitespace or borders (enclosure)?
Do your lines create a clear figure against a receding background (figure/ground)?
Are you working with these principles?

text:
Gestalt Principles in the Wild
With a partner, identify the Gestalt principles in the graphics on the worksheet.
For each chart, note:

Which principle(s) are at work
How the designer used it (spacing, color, enclosure, etc.)
Whether it helps or hurts the visualization

title: Lecture 8
text:
Geospatial data
Refers to information that identifies the location, shape, and relationships of objects on Earth using coordinates, maps, or geometry.
It combines location information (latitude/longitude), attribute information (descriptive details), and often temporal data (time-related context) to provide a complete geographic picture.

title: Lecture 8
text:
Vector Data

Represents discrete features
Types:

Points (e.g., schools, coordinates)
Lines (e.g., roads, rivers)
Polygons (e.g., countries, districts)

Best for bounded, discrete data

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
1.2 R Packages

# packages
library(tidyverse)     # ecosystem of data science packages
library(rvest)         # for web scraping
library(sf)            # "simple features" (classes for maps)
library(rnaturalearth) # maps database
library(leaflet)       # web interactive maps

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
1.3 Other Sources
Related to the above article, there is a similar one in SoFi’s website titled Average US Salary by State by Jacqueline DeMarco (updated Jan 28, 2025)
https://www.sofi.com/learn/content/average-salary-in-us/

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
2.1 Data from Forbes
The data in Forbes is an HTML table. However, this HTML table is not scrapable. This is why are providing a data set (obtained by copy-paste from Forbes’ webpage) in the text file wage.tsv (Tab-Separated Value file). The data can be easily imported with the function read_tsv() as follows:

# import file (all columns as 'character' data)
wages = read_tsv("wage.tsv", col_types = "ccc")

head(wages)

2 Alaska     $66,130               $31.79

4 Arkansas   $48,570               $23.35               
5 California $73,220               $35.20               
6 Colorado   $67,870               $32.63

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
2.2 Data from SoFi
Alternatively, you can also scrape the HTML table available in the SoFi webpage Average US Salary by State (by Jacqueline DeMarco, 03/28/24)

download.file(
  url = "https://www.sofi.com/learn/content/average-salary-in-us/", 
  destfile = "source/average-salary-in-us.html")

The interesting thing is that this table contains not just average salaries but also median salaries.

# import data
tbls = read_html("source/average-salary-in-us.html") |>
  html_table()

dat = tbls[[1]]

# data with average and median salaries
dat = dat |>
  mutate(Average = as.numeric(str_remove_all(Average, "[,$]")),
         Median = as.numeric(str_remove_all(Median, "[,$]")))

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
2.3 Data Preparation
The raw data is not quite ready to be crunched. We need to perform a few manipulations of Annual Average Wage values, dealing with strings:

wages = wages |>
  mutate(Average = as.numeric(str_remove_all(`Annual Average Wage`, "[$,]")))

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.1 Map of USA from "rnaturalearth"
We begin by producing a first map of the U.S. This requires to get map data of the US territory—at the State level—via ne_states(). Notice that the returnclass argument must be set as returnclass = "sf" to guarantee that the return map object is of class "sf" (simple features).

# map data
usa_map = ne_states(
  country = "united states of america", 
  returnclass = "sf")

The rnaturalearthhires package needs to be installed.
Installing the rnaturalearthhires package.
there is no package called 'pak'


Error in loadNamespace(x): there is no package called 'rnaturalearthhires'

class(usa_map)

Error: object 'usa_map' not found


As you can tell, the object usa_map is an object of class "sf". But it is also a data.frame object, i.e. a table. It contains a large number of columns. The most important columns—for our exploratory graphing purposes—are name (name of State), and the latitude-longitude coordinates in geometry.

names(usa_map)

Error: object 'usa_map' not found


With the "sf' object in place, we can pass it to ggplot() and its layer function geom_sf(). To get a map of the contiguous states, we adjust the latitude-longitude coordinates with coord_sf():

# basic map of USA (contiguous states)
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = usa_map) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-123, -67), ylim = c(25, 50))

Error: object 'usa_map' not found

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.2 Merging map-data with salary-data
So far we have a map of the US States. But what about the salary data? We need to merge (join) the table of the map usa_map with the table of average salary by state wages. This merging operation is done with inner_join(), indicating the names of the columns in both tables that have the States: name from table usa_map, and State from table wages

# merging map and wages data tables
usa_map_wages = inner_join(usa_map, wages, by = c("name" = "State"))

Error: object 'usa_map' not found

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.3 Version 1
With the merged data usa_map_wages, we can plot again a map of the US contiguous states, color coding the states with the Average values:

# version 1
usa_map_wages |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Average)) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-123, -67), ylim = c(25, 50))

Error: object 'usa_map_wages' not found

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.4 Version 2
We need to change the colors in order to follow the scheme used in the choropleth map published in Forbes:

# version 2
inner_join(usa_map, wages, by = c("name" = "State")) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Average)) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#0B057A", high = "#E6E6F1") +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-123, -67), ylim = c(25, 50))

Error: object 'usa_map' not found

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.5 Version 3
The next step involves categorizing salary values into different brackets

average salary greater than or equal to 70,000
average salary between 60,000 and 70,000
average salary between 50,000 and 60,000
average salary less than 50,000

Also, for sake of practice, we can change the color scheme (to pink):

# version 3
inner_join(usa_map, wages, by = c("name" = "State")) |>
  mutate(Cutoffs = case_when(
    Average >= 70000 ~ ">=70K",
    Average >= 60000 ~ "60K-70K",
    Average >= 50000 ~ "50K-60K",
    Average < 50000 ~ "<50K")) |>
  mutate(Color = case_when(
    Cutoffs == "<50K" ~ "#e6e6f1",
    Cutoffs == "50K-60K" ~ "#9D9BC9",
    Cutoffs == "60K-70K" ~ "#5450a1",
    Cutoffs == ">=70K" ~ "#0b057a"
  )) |>
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Color)) +
  # I don't look like this palette
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0b057a", "#5450a1", "#9D9BC9", "#e6e6f1")) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-123, -67), ylim = c(25, 50))

Error: object 'usa_map' not found

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.6 Map with all States
What if we want to visualize all States (including Alaska and Hawaii) and not just the contiguous territory?
No problem! We can use one of the maps from the package "usmap", namely the map object returned by the function us_map().
The following code chunk starts with the merging operation to join us_map() data with the wages data, and the pipes it to ggplot() and friends. In addition, we are using a Viridis continuous scale color:

# use the data from "us_map()" which is already an "sf" object
us_map() |>
  inner_join(wages, by = c("full" = "State")) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Average), color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
  theme_void()








We can make it interactive by using geom_sf_interactive() and activating the tooltip argument inside the aesthetic mappings.

# use the data from "us_map()" which is already an "sf" object
gmap = us_map() |>
  inner_join(wages, by = c("full" = "State")) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf_interactive(aes(fill = Average, 
                          tooltip = paste0(full, ": ", Average)), 
                      color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
  theme_void()

girafe(ggobj = gmap)

title: Average Salary by State, 2024
text:
3.7 Interactive Map with Leaflet
The previous maps have been produced with ggplot. They are nice but are static visualizations. Interestingly, we can use the "leaflet" package that allows us graph interactive web maps.

inner_join(usa_map, wages, by = c("name" = "State")) |>
  mutate(Cutoffs = case_when(
    Average >= 70000 ~ ">=70K",
    Average >= 60000 ~ "60K-70K",
    Average >= 50000 ~ "50K-60K",
    Average < 50000 ~ "<50K")) |>
  mutate(Color = case_when(
    Cutoffs == "<50K" ~ "#e6e6f1",
    Cutoffs == "50K-60K" ~ "#9D9BC9",
    Cutoffs == "60K-70K" ~ "#5450a1",
    Cutoffs == ">=70K" ~ "#0b057a"
  )) |>
  mutate(Label = paste0(name, ", $", Average)) |>
leaflet() |>
  addTiles() |>
  setView(lng = -100, 
          lat = 40, 
          zoom = 4) |>
  addPolygons(color = ~Color,
              fillColor = ~Color, 
              fillOpacity = 0.8, 
              label = ~Label,
              weight = 1,
              highlight = highlightOptions(
                weight = 5,
                color = "#000000",
                fillOpacity = 1))

title: Lecture 8
text:
Design principles


Use categorical scales for qualitative variables
Use sequential or diverging color scales for quantitative variables.
Avoid categorical color schemes for continuous data.
Color schemes must be interpretable and colorblind-friendly.
Beware of area bias: larger regions may dominate visually.
Normalize data where needed (e.g. per capita, not absolute counts).
